anchor.snippet: If a straight line intersects two other straight lines forming two interior angles on the same side
the two interior angles on the same side add to less than two right angles if and only if the two lines
If a straight line intersects two other straight lines that intersect on some side of first line
Euclidean geometry is a geometry that satisfies all of Euclid's axioms
Non-Euclidean geometries are geometries that do not satisfy the second form of the postulate
A hyperbolic geometry is a geometry that does not satisfy the original postulate
An elliptic geometry is geometry that does not satisfy the converse of the postulate

annotations.label: Spherical geometry: two lines meet in two points

anchor.snippet: in spherical geometry , two lines meet in exactly two points
is known as an absolute geometry
In a plane, given a line and a point not on it, at most one line parallel to the given line can be drawn through the point

annotations.label: Equivalence of Playfair's axiom with parallel postulate

anchor.snippet: in the presence of the remaining axioms which give Euclidean geometry, one can be used to prove the other

annotations.label: Statements equivalent to the parallel postulate

anchor.snippet: Many other statements equivalent to the parallel postulate have been suggested
If a straight line falling on two straight lines make the alternate angles equal to one another, the straight lines will be parallel to one another

annotations.label: Decomposition into Lotschnittaxiom and Aristotle's axiom

anchor.snippet: The parallel postulate is equivalent to the conjunction of the Lotschnittaxiom and of Aristotle's axiom
The former states that the perpendiculars to the sides of a right angle intersect
the latter states that there is no upper bound for the lengths of the distances from the leg of an angle to the other leg
Given three parallel lines, there is a line that intersects all three of them
there exists a line g which intersects a and m , but not n
